reason: The item’s core judgment (conservative vs non-conservative sizing when CO2 shows stronger inhibition in the hybrid case) is diagnostically valuable, but boundary integrity is weakened by having four correct choices, several of which are near-entailed paraphrases of the same inference (B and D) or expand into broader causal claims (F) that can feel under-specified without tighter quantification. Decoy quality is mixed: A is a clean single-fault overreach, but E’s error hinges on a subtle interpretation of “reduced effectiveness” versus “loss of additive effect,” which many experts may see as semantically ambiguous rather than structurally wrong, reducing discrimination.

The prompt tightly constrains the design-driver choice to the scenario with the highest stated inhibition threshold and requires a mechanism-based explanation that differentiates dynamic flame acceleration metrics from thermodynamic end-state pressure, giving strong diagnostic leverage. Decoy space is naturally enforced by the explicit thresholds (30% vs 35%) and the prohibition against “oxygen dilution only,” which helps separate shallow from expert reasoning. Minor ambiguity remains because it is a short-answer item without explicit scoring rubrics for acceptable kinetic/thermal mechanism variants or for how conservatism/margins above 35% should be treated.
